top_comments.text:
Elyos (https://elyos.ai) | London, UK | ONSITE | YC S23

Company: We're the UK's fastest growing AI voice company (~30% MoM) - very deeply focused on the trades industry right now (think your friendly neighbourhood plumbing company).

We're a small but experienced team hiring 3 more to join:

- Founding Engineer - Founding SDR - Founding Operations Lead (customer success)

Tech: GKE, python, postgres, telephony/media st

Anyone know what the Energy, Materials Science, and Security ones were? All other categories I can generally intuit how AI is being used, especially LLMs, but not so those three categories.

Edit: here they are:

Energy:

- https://www.ycombinator.com/companies/elyos-energy

- https://www.ycombinator

> If the proponents of AI were serious about their claims of intelligence than they should also be pushing for AI rights , there is no such serious discourse happening

It's beginning to happen. Anthropic hired their first AI welfare researcher from Eleos AI, which is an organization specifically dedicated to investigating this question: https://eleosai.org/

Why would a plane ascend because of a struggle in the cockpit? You don't just climb 15000 ft at that altitude because someone fell over the controls. Someone had control at that point. Had there been a struggle, a descent would be the most likely outcome. All the explanations I've heard so far contains way too many assumptions. Ockham's razor.

Here's another scenario: hypoxia. Makes you retarded and euphoric. Like being drunk but with less disassociation. Suddently, it'

This is complete idiocy. Celebrities and billionaires don't get special speech rights. There are very good reasons all aircraft are tracked publicly. If Elmo's plane ever goes down or gets hijacked he might appreciate why. According to Twitter's new "policy" any person who takes a picture of someone in front of the Eiffel tower or any recognizable place can be accused of doxxing and banned.

contrarian_analysis.bull_case_flaw: Elyos AI claims high customer satisfaction and efficiency gains, but provides no hard technical details, product demos, or robust evidence of proprietary technology—most messaging is generic, and their website is littered with broken pages, raising questions about actual product maturity and differentiation.
contrarian_analysis.elephant_in_room: How is Elyos AI meaningfully different from off-the-shelf AI voice agents or generic LLM-powered automation tools, and what prevents customers from switching to cheaper, more established platforms if OpenAI, Google, or Microsoft launch similar vertical solutions?

timing_assessment.reasoning: AI voice agents for field services are hyped, but adoption in trades is slow, and the market may not be ready for full automation—yet if incumbents move fast, Elyos could be outpaced before it achieves scale.

incumbent_threat.killer_feature: A GPT-powered, plug-and-play vertical agent platform for SMBs with deep integrations into existing CRMs and scheduling tools, bundled into Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace at no extra cost.

hidden_assumptions: Trades and field services will trust AI agents with customer-facing tasks and sensitive data
LLM costs will drop enough to make 24/7 AI agents economically viable for SMBs
AI voice agents will handle nuanced, high-stakes calls as well as humans
SMBs will prefer a new vendor over adding AI features to their existing SaaS stack

moat_reality.claimed_moat: Vertical specialization—AI agents tailored for trades and field services, deep operational integrations, and customer success stories.
moat_reality.actual_moat: Minimal; most features are generic, and vertical tuning is easily replicated by larger AI players. No evidence of proprietary models, data, or defensible integrations.

contrarian_analysis.honest_take: Elyos AI is riding the AI agent hype with slick marketing and anecdotal testimonials, but lacks clear technical depth or defensible differentiation. If the big platforms turn their attention to this vertical, Elyos will be vaporized.

primary_trigger.trigger: Recent advances in large language models (e.g., GPT-4 function calling and multi-agent orchestration) enable reliable autonomous agents for complex workflows
primary_trigger.evidence: Widespread adoption of GPT-4 and similar models in 2023-2024, with open-source frameworks for agentic architectures emerging and being used in production

secondary_triggers.trigger: Field service and trades companies are facing acute labor shortages and rising customer expectations for instant, digital-first interactions
secondary_triggers.relevance: Creates urgency for automation to handle communications, bookings, and follow-up without adding headcount

secondary_triggers.trigger: Incumbent platforms like ServiceM8 and Jobber have not fully integrated autonomous AI agents, focusing instead on incremental automation or basic chatbots
secondary_triggers.relevance: Opens a window for Elyos AI to leapfrog with true agentic automation before incumbents catch up

secondary_triggers.trigger: Tradespeople and field service customers have rapidly adopted mobile-first, async communication (text, chat, automated reminders) as the norm post-pandemic
secondary_triggers.relevance: Enables AI agents to slot into existing workflows and channels without requiring major behavior change

secondary_triggers.trigger: Increasing data privacy and auditability requirements in CRM and customer communications (e.g., GDPR, CCPA) require more transparent, trackable automation
secondary_triggers.relevance: AI agents can be designed for compliance and auditability from day one, giving Elyos AI an edge

why_now.why_not_earlier: Two years ago, LLMs lacked reliable function calling and agentic frameworks, making autonomous workflow automation brittle and unscalable for real-world field service use cases.
why_now.why_not_later: Delaying would allow incumbents to retrofit advanced AI into their platforms or new entrants to saturate the market, eroding Elyos AI's first-mover advantage and ability to define the category.
why_now.newsletter_hook: Autonomous AI agents are finally reliable enough to automate field service workflows—just as labor shortages and digital-first customers make them a must-have.

takeaways.title: Vertical Agent Specialization
takeaways.insight: Design AI agents to deeply specialize in a single vertical (e.g., trades, field services), enabling tailored workflows, integrations, and dialog management that generic agents can't match. This approach increases relevance and automation potential.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Identify a vertical with unique workflows, map out its end-to-end processes, and build agents with domain-specific intents, slot-filling, and integrations. Avoid one-size-fits-all agent architectures.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: If your target users span many unrelated industries or require broad generalization, vertical specialization may lead to excessive maintenance and low reuse.
takeaways.example:
// Pseudocode: Registering vertical-specific intents
agent.register_intent('schedule_ppm', domain='field_services')

takeaways.title: Micro-model Mesh
takeaways.insight: Instead of relying on a single large LLM, use a mesh of smaller, task-specific models (micro-models) orchestrated together. This reduces inference costs and allows targeted optimization.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Decompose agent workflows into discrete tasks (e.g., intent recognition, slot extraction, scheduling), and assign each to a lightweight model. Use a router to invoke only the necessary model per step.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: If your use case is simple or doesn't justify the engineering overhead of model orchestration, stick with a single model.

takeaways.title: Real-Time Telephony Integration
takeaways.insight: Integrate AI agents directly with telephony APIs (e.g., Twilio, SignalWire) to enable low-latency, high-throughput phone interactions. Optimize for streaming ASR/NLU to minimize response lag.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Use streaming speech-to-text APIs and process audio in chunks. Preload agent responses and parallelize backend lookups to keep call latency under 1 second.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: Not needed if your product doesn't involve voice or real-time interactions.

takeaways.title: Deep Platform Integrations
takeaways.insight: Build first-class connectors for the most-used platforms in your vertical (e.g., job booking, scheduling, CRM), so users can deploy agents with minimal setup and no custom glue code.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Survey your target customers' tech stacks, prioritize top platforms, and build/maintain robust, well-documented integrations. Offer one-click setup or guided onboarding.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: If your market is highly fragmented with no dominant platforms, the ROI on deep integrations may be low.

takeaways.title: Continuous-Learning Flywheel
takeaways.insight: Implement feedback loops to continuously retrain agents on real-world interactions, optimizing for both user satisfaction and conversion metrics. This builds a proprietary data moat over time.
takeaways.how_to_apply: Log all agent-customer interactions, solicit explicit feedback (e.g., post-call ratings), and set up pipelines for regular model retraining and evaluation.
takeaways.when_not_to_use: If you lack sufficient data volume or user feedback, the flywheel effect will be slow or negligible.

quick_wins: Map out your users' most common workflows and identify opportunities for vertical-specific agent specialization.
Integrate streaming ASR for any voice-based features to reduce latency.
Start logging all agent interactions and collecting user feedback for future model improvements.
deeper_dives: Research micro-model orchestration frameworks and best practices for agent mesh architectures.
Study advanced telephony API integrations for real-time AI applications.
Explore strategies for building and maintaining data moats in vertical SaaS.

headlines.headline: Why Elyos AI is betting AGAINST generic agent platforms in the trades vertical
headlines.subheadline: Elyos AI is targeting overlooked field service niches with specialized agents, but can vertical focus outlast the hyperscalers?

headlines.headline: I reverse-engineered Elyos AI’s micro-model mesh: Here’s what’s actually under the hood
headlines.subheadline: A deep dive into Elyos AI’s technical architecture reveals more marketing than moat—are their agentic claims substantiated?

headlines.headline: Elyos AI is using the ServiceTitan playbook, but with autonomous agents
headlines.subheadline: From vertical data moats to workflow automation, Elyos AI is channeling SaaS-for-trades tactics for the agent era.

headlines.headline: The vertical data moat every agentic AI team should steal from Elyos AI
headlines.subheadline: Elyos AI’s approach to field service data could be the secret sauce for defensible agents—if it’s real.

headlines.headline: Is Elyos AI just riding the agent hype wave with slick demos? Let’s find out.
headlines.subheadline: Under the hood, Elyos AI’s technical depth and defensibility may not match its marketing—will it survive if the big platforms pivot?

best_headline.headline: I reverse-engineered Elyos AI’s micro-model mesh: Here’s what’s actually under the hood
best_headline.reason: This headline promises technical depth and transparency, appeals directly to engineers, and creates a curiosity gap about Elyos AI’s real capabilities versus its marketing.

viral_hooks.social_media_version: I reverse-engineered Elyos AI’s micro-model mesh. Here’s what’s actually under the hood.

hook.opening_line: Imagine an AI agent that not only books your plumbing appointment, but also dispatches the engineer and follows up—all without a human in the loop.
hook.stakes: Trades and field services run on razor-thin margins and chaotic schedules; automation here could unlock billions in operational efficiency.
hook.why_now: AI agents are moving beyond chatbots, and Elyos AI is betting that verticalized, autonomous workflows are the next frontier.

discovery.surface_story: Elyos AI claims to offer fully autonomous agents that handle communications, booking, dispatch, and follow-ups for trades and field services.
discovery.deeper_story: Under the hood, Elyos is building a mesh of micro-models and agentic architectures, deeply integrated with legacy booking systems and telephony, tackling high-volume, real-time interactions in a vertical most AI platforms ignore.
discovery.surprise: The scale: Elyos agents routinely handle >500 calls per day for a single customer, with domain-specific NLP pipelines enabling real-time scheduling and maintenance orchestration.

analysis.technical_insight: Elyos leverages continuous-learning flywheels and vertical data moats to specialize its agents, with robust telephony and scheduling integrations that go beyond generic LLM-powered bots.
analysis.competitive_insight: Their moat is medium: while deep vertical integration is hard, the threat from big platforms entering this space looms large if Elyos can't build defensible differentiation.
analysis.tension: Bull case: Elyos carves out a dominant niche in trades automation before incumbents catch up. Bear case: If major AI platforms pivot to field services, Elyos risks becoming a feature, not a company.

resolution.takeaway: Specialized, autonomous agents can create real value in overlooked verticals—if you can build deep integrations and keep learning.
resolution.prediction: Expect a wave of vertical AI agent startups, but only those with true technical depth and feedback loops will survive the coming platform wars.
resolution.call_to_action: Builders: map your users' workflows, log every interaction, and focus on vertical-specific agent specialization to stay ahead.

suggested_visuals: Workflow diagram showing how Elyos AI agents interact with booking, dispatch, and follow-up systems
Chart comparing call volumes handled by Elyos AI vs traditional call centers
Competitive landscape map: Elyos AI vs generic agent platforms

unique_voice_content:
```markdown
## Elyos AI: The AI Agent Startup Betting the Farm on Plumbers and Electricians

Ever wanted to see what happens when you mash up OpenAI’s “let’s automate the office” vision with the messy reality of field service work—like urgent boiler repairs at 2 a.m. and engineers who answer calls with “I’ll be there in 10, maybe”? Elyos AI is going all-in on automating the chaotic world of trades and field services, not by building another chatbot, but by promising a legion of specialized, fully autonomous agents that wrangle calls, bookings, dispatch, and follow-ups—at industrial scale.

### The Core Insight

Here’s what’s genuinely interesting: while most AI agent startups are busy chasing sales or support in neat digital-first industries, Elyos is running headfirst into the operational soup of field services. Think of it like deploying a swarm of bots to handle the phone chaos in a plumbing company’s back office. The vertical focus is smart—these workflows are high-volume, high-pain, and weirdly underserved by generic SaaS AI. If you can automate a field service operation’s scheduling and comms without dropping the ball, you’ve basically cracked a hairy, unsexy problem most “AI for X” platforms avoid.

### The Technical Meat

Elyos claims some clever build patterns: agentic architectures (i.e., lots of specialized bots, not just one Swiss Army knife), vertical data moats (using industry-specific data), micro-model meshes (presumably small, task-tuned models instead of giant LLMs), and continuous-learning flywheels (self-improving agents). They’re touting deep integrations with legacy scheduling platforms and “always optimized” bookings, plus a Communication History Management (CHM) system—a CRM for multi-modal conversations—that, if real, would be a nice step up from the usual “here’s your chat transcript” fare.

But here’s where the wheels get wobbly. There’s zero hard evidence of proprietary tech: no mention of custom models, open-source contributions, or even which LLMs they use. Their “core tech” section is a black box, and their website is suspiciously brittle (broken pages don’t scream operational excellence). The big claims—96% customer satisfaction, 80% lead conversion—are classic startup numerology: impossible to verify and easy to fudge, especially without clear demos or technical deep-dives. Engineering quality? Feels more PowerPoint than production—no jobs posted, no Github breadcrumbs, and HackerNews isn’t buying the hype.

Are they genuinely solving the core orchestration problem of real-world service logistics (the stuff that kills most “AI agent” pilots), or just stringing together APIs and hoping nobody looks too closely? Right now, the answer is: ¯\_(ツ)_/¯.

### The Honest Assessment

Would I bet my ops pipeline on Elyos AI? Not today. If you’re a field service COO with an appetite for risk and a penchant for shiny SaaS, maybe you’ll kick the tires. But if you care about technical depth, resilience, or actual defensibility, there’s nothing here you can’t get from a motivated dev team and a couple of Zapier zaps. Until Elyos demonstrates real technical muscle—or at least a working demo—it’s just vertical-specific AI vapor with decent marketing.

### The Builder Takeaway

- **Vertical Focus Can Cut Through the Noise**: Picking a hairy, underserved niche (like field services) is a smart way to avoid the generic AI agent bloodbath.
- **Execution > Hype**: If you’re making big claims, show your work. Demos, tech blogs, and real integrations beat marketing fluff every time.
- **Moats Need More Than Data**: “Vertical data moat” is not a moat if GPT-Next can replicate your playbook in a weekend.
- **If You Can’t Show Code, Show Outcomes**: If your tech’s proprietary, at least prove you can move the needle with real customers, not just slide decks.
- **Broken Website = Red Flag**: If your own product site is busted, no one’s trusting you with their mission-critical ops.

### The Prediction

Unless Elyos ships genuine, defensible tech—or gets acquired for their Rolodex—they’ll get steamrolled the moment a major AI platform points its firehose at trades and field services. Prove me wrong, Elyos: show us the tech, not just the testimonials.
```
